refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire volumetric or mass flow data from material handling subsystems during operation.
Calculate throughput rate (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired flow data.
Display or transmit throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
Log historical throughput data for post-operation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Continuous volumetric flow measurement via inline sensor with real-time signal conditioning and local data buffering
DP2: Periodic mass-based sampling with calibrated weighing and extrapolation to throughput rate over fixed intervals
DP3: Indirect throughput inference from operational parameters (e.g., motor speed, cycle time, material density) with lookup tables or empirical models
DP4: Hybrid dual-path measurement (volumetric + mass cross-check) with automated reconciliation logic to flag anomalies
DP5: Time-stamped event logging of material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) with post-processing aggregation to compute rates
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Hybrid dual-path measurement (volumetric + mass cross-check) with automated reconciliation logic to flag anomalies

potential_sources: Volumetric and mass measurement subsystems must share a common time reference and synchronization protocol to ensure valid cross-checks; loose coupling requires explicit interface contract (timestamp precision, latency bounds).

Reconciliation logic depends on material density assumptions; if density varies significantly across material types or conditions, the DP must accommodate parameterization or real-time density input to avoid false anomaly flags.
Data buffering and reporting cadence must be decoupled from measurement acquisition to prevent measurement latency from blocking throughput reporting; asynchronous queue or ring buffer recommended.
Safety-critical thresholds (e.g., overspeed, underflow) must be independent of reporting logic to ensure fail-safe behavior even if reporting subsystem fails.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 is selected because it provides redundancy and cross-validation, improving confidence in reported productivity against acceptance criteria. Coupling risks are manageable through explicit interface specifications and parameterization. Single-path DPs (DP1, DP2, DP3) offer lower complexity but higher risk of systematic measurement bias or single-point failure, which could invalidate acceptance testing.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material density is known or can be inferred with acceptable accuracy. Risk: If density varies unpredictably, reconciliation logic may produce false anomalies. Mitigation: Require density input as a configurable parameter or sensor input; document acceptable density range.

Assumption: Both volumetric and mass measurement subsystems have comparable accuracy and response time. Risk: If one subsystem is significantly slower or noisier, reconciliation may be unreliable. Mitigation: Specify measurement accuracy and latency requirements in DP-to-IM mapping; validate during design review.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits installation of inline measurement sensors without disrupting material flow or creating pinch/entanglement hazards. Risk: Sensor placement may conflict with safety requirements. Mitigation: Coordinate with safety plan early; consider non-intrusive measurement alternatives (e.g., load cells, optical sensors) if inline sensors are prohibited.

Assumption: Operational policies define acceptable reporting latency and update frequency. Risk: If real-time reporting is required but measurement buffering introduces delay, acceptance criteria may not be met. Mitigation: Establish explicit latency budget in acceptance criteria; validate during pilot operations.

Assumption: Productivity target is stable over the evaluation period and not affected by external factors (e.g., material quality, operator skill, equipment wear). Risk: If target is not achievable due to systemic constraints, measurement system will be blamed. Mitigation: Baseline productivity measurement before system deployment; document known sources of variability.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Required measurement accuracy (e.g., ±5% of target throughput), (2) Reporting latency and update frequency (e.g., rolling 5-minute average, updated every 30 seconds), (3) Material types and density ranges expected during evaluation, (4) Safety constraints on sensor placement and measurement method, (5) Definition of 'representative operations' (e.g., steady-state, ramp-up, shutdown phases). These refinements will enable DP-to-IM mapping to specify sensor types, data acquisition hardware, and reconciliation algorithm parameters without ambiguity. Additionally, clarify whether productivity_target is absolute (fixed throughput rate) or relative (percentage of theoretical maximum), as this affects how measurement uncertainty propagates to acceptance criteria.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement reconciliation and anomaly detection engine that integrates volumetric and mass-based cross-checks with automated flagging logic

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives volumetric measurements and mass data streams from Vehicle sensors

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs reconciliation results and anomaly flags to downstream consumers

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits critical anomaly flags when measurement discrepancies exceed safety thresholds

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives reconciliation configuration and anomaly threshold parameters

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: measurement reconciliation and anomaly detection are core NGHE functions. Dual-path logic (volumetric + mass) naturally centralizes here. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry I/O and safety alert dispatch escape boundary.

Poor fit: Vehicle owns sensor hardware, not reconciliation logic. Placing DP here couples vehicle firmware to complex anomaly algorithms, violates separation of concerns. Reconciliation logic would diffuse across vehicle control stack.

Poor fit: Teleoperation handles remote command/control, not measurement validation. Placing reconciliation here creates tight coupling between operator interface and sensor logic. High diffusion into operator workflows.

Moderate-weak fit: Safety Services owns alert dispatch but not measurement reconciliation. Placing dual-path logic here conflates measurement validation with safety response. Diffusion: reconciliation logic leaks into safety decision-making domain.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP4 (hybrid dual-path measurement reconciliation with automated anomaly flagging) is a measurement integrity and data quality function that belongs in the NGHE Solution layer. NGHE is the logical owner of sensor data fusion, cross-validation, and anomaly detection. The DP is cohesive within NGHE because both volumetric and mass measurement streams flow into NGHE for processing, and reconciliation results feed downstream tasking and safety systems. Diffusion is minimal: only standard telemetry I/O and safety alert interfaces cross the boundary.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle sensors are not reliably time-synchronized, reconciliation logic may require Vehicle-side buffering or timestamp correction, creating hidden coupling back to Vehicle firmware.
Risk: If anomaly thresholds are highly vehicle-specific (e.g., different for different payload types), DP may need to diffuse configuration logic into Vehicle or Tasking modules.
Risk: If real-time latency requirements are strict, NGHE-based centralized reconciliation may introduce unacceptable delay; may require partial logic migration to Vehicle edge.
Risk: If Safety Services requires direct access to raw measurement streams for independent validation, DP may need to expose internal measurement data, increasing coupling.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP4 is well-formulated and containment is strong. No revision needed. Ensure that: (1) Telemetry interface carries both volumetric and mass measurements with synchronized timestamps; (2) Tasking interface allows dynamic threshold updates without redeployment; (3) SafetyAlert interface is unidirectional (NGHE → Safety Services) to prevent feedback loops; (4) NGHE reconciliation logic is stateless or uses short-lived state to avoid memory diffusion across vehicle sessions.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 (hybrid dual-path measurement with automated reconciliation and anomaly flagging) directly addresses FR requirement to enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time. The dual-path approach with cross-check logic provides both measurement modalities and internal validation, satisfying the functional intent. Logic is sound and testable.

diffusion_risks: Time-synchronization dependency: If Vehicle sensors lack reliable timestamp alignment, reconciliation logic may require hidden coupling back to Vehicle firmware for buffering or correction, creating implicit DP diffusion.

Vehicle-specific threshold configuration: If anomaly thresholds vary significantly by payload type or vehicle configuration, threshold management logic may diffuse into Vehicle or Tasking modules, fragmenting DP ownership.

Real-time latency constraint: Centralized NGHE reconciliation may introduce unacceptable delay under strict latency requirements, forcing partial logic migration to Vehicle edge layer and splitting DP across boundaries.

Safety Services raw data access: If Safety Services requires independent validation via direct raw measurement streams, NGHE must expose internal state, increasing coupling and creating secondary diffusion path.

State management across sessions: If reconciliation maintains session-level state (e.g., historical baselines, drift correction), state diffusion across vehicle lifecycle may violate module isolation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution containment score of 0.92 is strong. Measurement reconciliation and dual-path logic are cohesive core functions naturally owned by NGHE. Volumetric and mass data streams converge at NGHE for processing; reconciliation results flow outbound to Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Tasking. Minimal boundary escape (0.08 diffusion) limited to standard I/O interfaces. Alternative placements (Vehicle 0.35, Teleoperation 0.28, Safety Services 0.45) all show poor fit and high diffusion. Recommended ownership is sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period.' Measurement accuracy and anomaly detection can be validated by comparing NGHE reconciliation outputs against ground-truth throughput data collected during representative operations. Anomaly flagging can be verified by injecting known measurement discrepancies and confirming SafetyAlert dispatch. Latency and data quality metrics are measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear. NGHE Solution owns measurement reconciliation logic (DP4 realization). Vehicle owns sensor hardware and telemetry transmission (upstream). Tasking owns threshold configuration and parameter updates (control input). Safety Services owns alert response and safety decision-making (downstream consumer). Interfaces are unidirectional or request-response, reducing ambiguity. However, clarity depends on explicit interface contracts (timestamp format, data schema, threshold update protocol) being documented and enforced.

recommended_revisions: Formalize telemetry interface contract: Specify mandatory timestamp synchronization mechanism (e.g., NTP, GPS time, or Vehicle-provided clock reference) to eliminate hidden coupling risk. Define acceptable clock skew tolerance for reconciliation logic.

Decouple threshold management: Implement dynamic threshold update capability via Tasking interface without requiring NGHE redeployment. Use configuration versioning to track threshold changes and enable rollback.

Enforce SafetyAlert unidirectionality: Ensure SafetyAlert interface is strictly outbound (NGHE → Safety Services). Prohibit feedback or override signals from Safety Services back to NGHE to prevent control loops and state diffusion.

Define state lifecycle: Clarify whether reconciliation state (e.g., baseline drift, historical anomaly counts) persists across vehicle sessions or resets. If persistent, specify storage ownership (NGHE or external state service) and lifecycle management to avoid implicit diffusion.

Latency budget allocation: Measure end-to-end latency from sensor reading to SafetyAlert dispatch. If latency exceeds operational safety requirements, document trade-off decision (accept delay, migrate logic to Vehicle edge, or reduce reconciliation complexity). Do not allow silent latency creep.

Raw measurement data access policy: If Safety Services requires independent validation, define a separate read-only telemetry stream (distinct from reconciliation output) to prevent Safety Services from coupling to NGHE internal state. Maintain separation of concerns.

Add acceptance criterion for anomaly detection: Supplement productivity target criterion with explicit anomaly detection accuracy metric (e.g., 'false positive rate ≤ 5%, false negative rate ≤ 2%') to ensure DP4 reconciliation logic meets safety and operational expectations.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with strong containment (0.92) and clear allocation. However, the gate results expose seven material diffusion risks and seven recommended revisions that are architectural in nature and require decomposition to resolve. The high safety criticality of the system and the explicit latency, state management, and interface coupling risks demand that we decompose FR4 into sub-level FRs that isolate these concerns as distinct functional requirements. This prevents risks from being buried in implementation details and ensures each risk is addressed by explicit design intent rather than post-hoc mitigation. Decomposition is justified because: (1) DP4 is logically sound but architecturally complex (dual-path reconciliation + anomaly flagging + state management + interface contracts); (2) diffusion risks cluster around orthogonal concerns (time-sync, threshold config, latency, state lifecycle, safety decoupling); (3) safety criticality demands explicit traceability from each risk to a corresponding FR; (4) recommended revisions are functional in nature (e.g., 'formalize telemetry interface contract,' 'decouple threshold management,' 'enforce SafetyAlert unidirectionality') and should be elevated to sub-FR level to ensure they are designed, not assumed.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Enable time-synchronized dual-path measurement (volumetric and mass) with explicit clock reference and acceptable skew tolerance to eliminate hidden coupling to Vehicle firmware.
FR4.2: Reconcile volumetric and mass measurement streams with cross-check logic and flag anomalies when discrepancy exceeds tolerance, independent of threshold configuration.
FR4.3: Support dynamic anomaly threshold configuration and updates via Tasking interface without requiring NGHE redeployment or state reset.
FR4.4: Dispatch SafetyAlert signals unidirectionally (NGHE → Safety Services) with no feedback or override coupling to prevent control loops and state diffusion.

FR4.5: Manage reconciliation state (baseline drift, historical anomaly counts) with explicit lifecycle definition (persistent vs. session-reset) and clear ownership (NGHE-internal vs. external state service).
FR4.6: Ensure end-to-end latency from sensor reading to SafetyAlert dispatch remains within operational safety budget; document trade-off decisions if latency exceeds requirements.
FR4.7: Provide read-only raw measurement telemetry stream to Safety Services for independent validation, decoupled from reconciliation output and internal state.

revise.why: FR4 as currently stated ('Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time') is functionally correct but architecturally underspecified. It does not explicitly capture the seven orthogonal concerns (time-sync, threshold decoupling, latency, state lifecycle, safety unidirectionality, raw data access, anomaly accuracy) that the gate analysis identified as diffusion risks. Revising FR4 to include these concerns as explicit sub-FRs ensures that architectural risks are elevated to functional intent and traced through design and verification, rather than left as implementation assumptions. This is especially critical given high safety criticality.

assumptions: Vehicle sensors provide timestamps with acceptable clock skew (to be quantified in FR4.1 decomposition).
Tasking module can issue threshold configuration updates asynchronously without blocking NGHE measurement pipeline.
Safety Services can tolerate unidirectional alert dispatch without requiring feedback or override capability.
Reconciliation state (if persistent) can be managed by NGHE without external state service dependency.
End-to-end latency budget is defined by Safety and Operational requirements (to be confirmed in FR4.6 decomposition).
Raw measurement data stream can be exposed to Safety Services without violating data privacy or security constraints.
open_risks: Time-synchronization dependency: Vehicle sensors may lack reliable timestamp alignment; NTP or GPS time reference may not be available in all deployment contexts.
Vehicle-specific threshold configuration: Anomaly thresholds may vary by payload type or vehicle configuration, risking diffusion of threshold logic into Vehicle or Tasking modules.
Real-time latency constraint: Centralized NGHE reconciliation may introduce unacceptable delay under strict latency requirements, forcing partial logic migration to Vehicle edge layer.
Safety Services raw data access: If Safety Services requires independent validation, NGHE must expose internal state, increasing coupling and creating secondary diffusion path.
State management across sessions: Reconciliation state (e.g., historical baselines, drift correction) may violate module isolation if not explicitly managed.

Anomaly detection accuracy: False positive and false negative rates for anomaly flagging are not yet specified; high false positive rate may trigger excessive SafetyAlerts; high false negative rate may miss genuine safety events.
Interface contract enforcement: Timestamp format, data schema, threshold update protocol, and SafetyAlert signal format must be formally specified and enforced to prevent hidden coupling.
evidence_needed: Specification of time-synchronization mechanism (NTP, GPS, Vehicle clock reference) and acceptable clock skew tolerance for reconciliation logic.
Specification of dynamic threshold update protocol and configuration versioning scheme to decouple threshold management from NGHE deployment.
Measurement of end-to-end latency from sensor reading to SafetyAlert dispatch under representative operational conditions; comparison against safety and operational latency budgets.
Definition of reconciliation state lifecycle (persistent vs. session-reset) and ownership (NGHE-internal vs. external state service).
Specification of anomaly detection accuracy metrics (false positive rate, false negative rate) and acceptance criteria.

Formal interface contracts for telemetry input (timestamp format, data schema, clock reference), threshold configuration input (update protocol, versioning), SafetyAlert output (signal format, unidirectionality enforcement), and raw measurement telemetry output (data schema, access control).
Verification plan for each sub-FR (FR4.1–FR4.7) including acceptance criteria, test scenarios, and ground-truth data collection methodology.
Safety analysis (FMEA or similar) for each sub-FR to confirm that decomposition does not introduce new failure modes or increase risk.
